stem: A social media application stores user posts in DynamoDB. A 'trending posts' feature queries the 100 most-liked posts in the last hour. This query runs thousands of times per minute and causes hot partition issues on the likes-count attribute. Which solution resolves this with the lowest latency?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable DynamoDB Streams to maintain a sorted set of trending posts in a Lambda function

options.label: B
options.text: Use DynamoDB DAX to cache trending post query results

options.label: C
options.text: Add a global secondary index on the likes-count attribute

options.label: D
options.text: Migrate the trending posts data to Amazon ElastiCache Redis and refresh every minute

explanation: Trending posts are computed aggregates (top 100 by likes in the last hour) — not individual item lookups. ElastiCache Redis sorted sets are ideal: a background job updates the sorted set every minute from DynamoDB data, and the application reads from Redis at microsecond latency with no DynamoDB load for this query. DAX caches individual item lookups (GetItem, Query) but not computed aggregates — the trending query still hits DynamoDB on cache miss. A GSI on likes-count still reads DynamoDB on every request. DynamoDB Streams + Lambda is complex and introduces replication lag.

stem: A company runs a machine learning training pipeline on EC2. Training jobs run for 6-12 hours and read 50 TB of training data stored in S3. The pipeline team observes that S3 GET requests are the bottleneck, accounting for 70% of training time. Which solution most improves training throughput?

options.label: A
options.text: Copy the training data to EBS io2 volumes on each training instance

options.label: B
options.text: Use Amazon FSx for Lustre integrated with S3 to provide a high-throughput parallel file system

options.label: C
options.text: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the training data bucket

options.label: D
options.text: Use S3 Byte-Range Fetch to parallelize downloads within each training job

explanation: FSx for Lustre is a high-performance parallel file system purpose-built for ML training workloads. Its S3 integration allows the Lustre file system to be linked to the S3 bucket — data is lazily loaded on first access and cached for subsequent epochs. FSx for Lustre delivers hundreds of GB/s throughput. S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes upload/download to S3 but the bottleneck is the distributed GET rate, not routing. Copying 50 TB to EBS per training instance is cost-prohibitive and time-consuming. Byte-Range Fetch helps parallelize within a single instance but doesn't address multi-instance bottlenecks.

stem: An API handles 5,000 requests per second during peak hours. Each request invokes a Lambda function that makes a database call to RDS PostgreSQL. Lambda is scaling to 3,000 concurrent functions, exhausting the RDS max_connections limit (500), causing connection refused errors. Which solution resolves this with minimal application changes?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the RDS instance size to a db.r5.8xlarge to increase max_connections

options.label: B
options.text: Switch from RDS to DynamoDB to eliminate connection limits

options.label: C
options.text: Enable RDS Proxy between Lambda and RDS to pool and multiplex database connections

options.label: D
options.text: Use ElastiCache to cache all database queries and eliminate RDS calls

explanation: RDS Proxy pools connections at the proxy layer, multiplexing thousands of Lambda concurrent connections into the RDS max_connections limit. Lambda functions connect to the proxy endpoint (not RDS directly), and the proxy manages a connection pool that RDS can handle. This is a drop-in change (update the Lambda connection string) with no application logic changes. Increasing the instance size marginally increases max_connections (still limited to ~500-1000). Switching to DynamoDB requires application rewrite. ElastiCache caching helps for reads but doesn't address write/transactional query connection exhaustion.

stem: A company's Redshift cluster has a mix of short dashboard queries (1-2 seconds) and long analytical queries (5-10 minutes). The long queries are consuming all cluster resources and causing dashboard queries to queue for minutes. Which Redshift feature resolves resource contention?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for short and long queries

options.label: B
options.text: Create separate Redshift clusters for dashboard and analytical workloads

options.label: C
options.text: Enable Redshift Spectrum to offload long queries to S3

options.label: D
options.text: Scale up the Redshift cluster to a larger node type

explanation: Redshift WLM allows defining query queues with separate memory allocation percentages and concurrency limits. Short queries (dashboard) go to a high-concurrency, low-memory queue; long analytical queries go to a separate queue with high memory but low concurrency. This prevents long queries from starving dashboard queries. Scaling up the cluster doesn't separate workloads — long queries still dominate. Redshift Spectrum offloads queries to S3 data but doesn't automatically route by query duration. Separate clusters are expensive and require ETL to keep data synchronized.

stem: A company processes IoT sensor data with Kinesis Data Streams. The stream has 10 shards and a single Lambda consumer. Processing latency is growing as sensor data volume increases. The team adds more shards but latency does not improve. What is the most likely cause and solution?

options.label: A
options.text: Lambda cold starts are causing latency; enable Provisioned Concurrency

options.label: B
options.text: The Lambda function is processing one shard sequentially; add more Lambda functions as consumers

options.label: C
options.text: Kinesis standard consumers have a 2 MB/s per-shard read limit shared across all consumers

options.label: D
options.text: The Kinesis retention period is too short; increase it to 7 days to allow reprocessing

explanation: Standard Kinesis consumers (GetRecords API) share the 2 MB/s per-shard read throughput. With a single consumer and 10 shards, this isn't the issue — but if multiple consumers exist or the Lambda function polls slowly, records queue up. Enhanced Fan-Out (RegisterStreamConsumer) gives each registered consumer its own dedicated 2 MB/s per shard via HTTP/2 push, eliminating read sharing. However, if there's a single Lambda consumer, the root cause may be Lambda processing throughput — but the Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out option is the answer AWS tests most frequently for this scenario. Cold starts affect initial latency, not sustained throughput growth.

stem: A company runs a read-heavy e-commerce product catalog on RDS MySQL. The catalog has 10 million products and receives 50,000 reads per second during peak hours. Adding read replicas has not fully resolved the bottleneck because product details change infrequently (once per day on average). Which additional solution provides the best read performance improvement?

options.label: A
options.text: Add more RDS read replicas in the same region

options.label: B
options.text: Migrate to Aurora MySQL for higher read throughput

options.label: C
options.text: Implement ElastiCache for Memcached with a cache-aside pattern for product catalog reads

options.label: D
options.text: Enable RDS Performance Insights to identify slow queries

explanation: Product details that change once per day are ideal for caching. ElastiCache Memcached with cache-aside: the application checks the cache first; on miss, reads from RDS and populates the cache with a TTL of 24 hours. This absorbs the majority of 50K reads/second from the cache layer (sub-millisecond latency) rather than hitting RDS at all. Additional read replicas add replication lag and cost but still process each read at database level. Performance Insights identifies slow queries (detective) but doesn't reduce load. Aurora MySQL improves read throughput but still requires cache for 50K RPS.

stem: A data engineering team builds a real-time fraud detection pipeline. Each financial transaction must be scored within 100ms. The pipeline receives 20,000 transactions per second. The scoring model requires looking up customer transaction history for the last 30 days. Which architecture meets the latency requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Stream transactions through Kinesis → Lambda (query ElastiCache Redis for pre-computed customer profiles) → publish fraud score

options.label: B
options.text: Stream transactions through Kinesis → Lambda (query RDS for history) → publish fraud score

options.label: C
options.text: Use SQS FIFO to serialize transactions per customer → Lambda scoring function

options.label: D
options.text: Batch transactions in Kinesis Firehose → S3 → Athena query for fraud scoring

explanation: At 100ms latency and 20K TPS, the lookup must complete in single-digit milliseconds. Pre-computed customer profiles in ElastiCache Redis (updated by a background job) provide sub-millisecond lookups. Lambda queries Redis for the profile, runs the scoring model, and returns results within the 100ms budget. Querying RDS in real-time at 20K TPS would hit connection and query latency limits. Kinesis Firehose is for batch delivery to S3, not real-time processing. SQS FIFO serializes per customer (low throughput) and doesn't meet the 100ms real-time requirement.

stem: A company wants to provide a search feature on their e-commerce website that supports full-text search, faceted filtering (by price range, brand, category), autocomplete suggestions, and typo tolerance. Which AWS service is best suited for this use case?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon Kendra for intelligent enterprise search

options.label: B
options.text: Amazon DynamoDB with a GSI on searchable attributes

options.label: C
options.text: Amazon OpenSearch Service with a product catalog index

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon RDS with full-text search indexes

explanation: OpenSearch Service is purpose-built for full-text search with: inverted index for text search, aggregations for faceted filtering (price range buckets, brand terms), completion suggester for autocomplete, and fuzzy matching for typo tolerance. These are native OpenSearch features configured at index creation. RDS full-text search is limited in scalability and lacks faceted aggregations. DynamoDB GSIs support key-value lookups, not full-text or fuzzy search. Kendra is designed for enterprise document search (FAQs, document repositories) using NLP — not product catalog search with faceted filters.

stem: A company uses EBS gp2 volumes for their database server. During peak hours, IOPS are consistently hitting the gp2 burst limit, causing I/O latency spikes. The volumes are 1 TB each. How should the solutions architect resolve this?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable EBS Multi-Attach to share the volume across multiple instances

options.label: B
options.text: Modify the volumes from gp2 to gp3 and set a provisioned IOPS value above the gp2 burst limit

options.label: C
options.text: Add more 1 TB gp2 volumes and use RAID 0 to aggregate IOPS

options.label: D
options.text: Take a snapshot and restore to a larger gp2 volume to get more baseline IOPS

explanation: gp3 volumes allow independent configuration of IOPS (up to 16,000) and throughput regardless of volume size, without the burst model of gp2. Modifying in-place from gp2 to gp3 is an online operation (no downtime) and gp3 baseline IOPS (3,000 included, up to 16,000 provisioned) is always available — no burst credits to exhaust. RAID 0 with multiple gp2 volumes works but is complex and the burst limit applies per volume. Increasing gp2 volume size raises baseline IOPS (3 IOPS/GB) but a 1TB gp2 has 3,000 IOPS which may still be insufficient. EBS Multi-Attach is for io1/io2 cluster applications, not IOPS optimization.

stem: A company's application performs complex graph traversals — finding all friends-of-friends within 3 hops for a social network with 100 million users. Current RDS queries for this take 30+ seconds. Which AWS service is optimized for this use case?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon Redshift for parallel analytical queries

options.label: B
options.text: Amazon DynamoDB with adjacency list design pattern

options.label: C
options.text: Amazon ElastiCache with a custom graph data structure in Redis

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Neptune for native graph traversal queries

explanation: Amazon Neptune is a purpose-built graph database that natively supports property graphs (Gremlin/TinkerPop) and RDF (SPARQL). Graph traversals like friends-of-friends-of-friends are optimized at the storage and query engine level in Neptune, executing in milliseconds to seconds vs. 30+ seconds in relational joins. DynamoDB adjacency list patterns work for simple graph lookups but require application-level traversal logic that degrades at depth. Redshift is optimized for analytical aggregations, not graph traversal. Redis custom structures require custom code for graph operations at scale.

stem: A company runs a serverless API using API Gateway and Lambda. During promotional events, the API receives sudden 10x traffic spikes. The Lambda functions are invoking downstream services that can handle 1,000 RPS maximum. Lambda concurrency has no limit set. What should the solutions architect configure to protect the downstream services?

options.label: A
options.text: Set a Lambda reserved concurrency limit equal to the downstream service's maximum capacity

options.label: B
options.text: Add an SQS queue between API Gateway and Lambda to buffer requests

options.label: C
options.text: Enable API Gateway caching to serve repeated requests without invoking Lambda

options.label: D
options.text: Enable API Gateway throttling at the stage level and set Lambda provisioned concurrency

explanation: Reserved concurrency sets a hard cap on the number of concurrent Lambda executions for a function. If the downstream service handles 1,000 RPS and Lambda processes requests in ~1 second on average, setting reserved concurrency to 1,000 limits Lambda to 1,000 concurrent executions — matching the downstream capacity. API Gateway returns 429 (throttle) for requests beyond this limit, protecting the downstream. API Gateway caching only helps for cacheable GET requests, not all API calls. SQS buffering decouples but adds latency and complexity for synchronous APIs. Stage throttling limits TPS at the API level but doesn't enforce concurrent downstream protection.

stem: A company is building a data lake on S3. The data engineering team needs to catalog all S3 data with schema inference, and data analysts need to query the data using standard SQL without managing any servers. Which combination of services implements this architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: S3 + DMS for schema migration + Aurora Serverless for queries

options.label: B
options.text: S3 + Redshift COPY command for ingestion, Redshift for SQL queries

options.label: C
options.text: S3 + AWS Glue Crawler for cataloging + Amazon Athena for serverless SQL queries

options.label: D
options.text: S3 + AWS Lake Formation + Amazon EMR for Spark SQL queries

explanation: The canonical serverless data lake architecture: Glue Crawlers scan S3 data and infer schemas (Parquet, CSV, JSON, ORC), populating the Glue Data Catalog. Athena uses the Data Catalog to execute standard SQL queries directly on S3 — no servers, no cluster provisioning, pay per query. Redshift requires loading data via COPY and managing a cluster. Lake Formation + EMR provides fine-grained access control and Spark processing but requires cluster management — not 'no servers.' DMS + Aurora Serverless is a database migration pattern, not a data lake query pattern.

stem: An application generates PDF reports by running a CPU-intensive process that takes 25 minutes per report. The process is triggered via an API call. Users should receive a callback notification when the report is ready. Lambda cannot be used because of the 15-minute limit. Which serverless architecture handles this?

options.label: A
options.text: API Gateway → Step Functions state machine → EC2 instance → SNS notification

options.label: B
options.text: Increase Lambda timeout to 30 minutes for this specific function

options.label: C
options.text: API Gateway → SQS → ECS Fargate task (triggered by SQS event) → SNS notification.

options.label: D
options.text: API Gateway → Lambda (start process) → EC2 Spot instance → S3 when complete

explanation: SQS + Fargate is the correct pattern for jobs exceeding Lambda's 15-minute limit: the API call enqueues a message in SQS; ECS Fargate tasks are triggered by the queue (via ECS task queue consumers or EventBridge Pipes); Fargate runs the 25-minute PDF generation; upon completion, the task publishes to SNS which triggers the user callback. This is fully serverless (no EC2 management). Lambda timeouts cannot exceed 15 minutes. Step Functions + EC2 requires managing EC2 instances. API Gateway → Lambda → EC2 still requires EC2 management and the Spot interruption risk.

stem: A company migrates a monolithic application to microservices on ECS. Service-to-service calls are increasing network latency and the team needs service discovery so services can find each other dynamically as tasks scale and IP addresses change. Which AWS service provides this?

options.label: A
options.text: An Application Load Balancer for each microservice with a fixed DNS name

options.label: B
options.text: AWS Cloud Map for service discovery with DNS and health checking

options.label: C
options.text: AWS App Mesh with Envoy sidecars for service discovery

options.label: D
options.text: Route 53 public hosted zones for each service

explanation: AWS Cloud Map is the purpose-built service registry and discovery service for cloud-native applications. Services register their ECS tasks with Cloud Map (including IP, port, health status), and other services discover them via DNS or API. As tasks scale up/down and IP addresses change, Cloud Map automatically updates its registry. Route 53 public hosted zones expose services publicly — not appropriate for internal microservices. An ALB per microservice works but adds cost and management overhead for simple service discovery. App Mesh provides a service mesh (traffic management, observability) — it uses Cloud Map for service discovery internally but adds significant operational complexity for this use case.

stem: A company uses Aurora MySQL with a db.r5.4xlarge writer instance. Write latency has increased as the write volume has grown to 80,000 writes per second. The solutions architect needs to scale write throughput. Which option is valid for Aurora MySQL?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable Aurora Multi-Master for multiple writer instances

options.label: B
options.text: Scale up to a larger instance type (db.r5.12xlarge) and optimize the write-heavy queries

options.label: C
options.text: Shard the database by splitting the write-heavy tables across multiple Aurora clusters

options.label: D
options.text: Add Aurora read replicas to distribute write load across multiple instances

explanation: Aurora MySQL Multi-Master was deprecated. Aurora's architecture has a single writer — read replicas receive write forwarding but do not independently accept writes. The only valid options for exceeding a single Aurora instance's write throughput are: (1) vertical scaling (up to db.r5.24xlarge/db.r6g.16xlarge), or (2) horizontal sharding (splitting data across multiple Aurora clusters). At 80K writes/second, a larger instance type may help but sharding is the architecturally correct answer for sustained horizontal write scaling. Read replicas cannot process writes independently. Multi-Master is not available for new clusters.

stem: A company wants to analyze application logs stored in S3 using SQL. Log files are in JSON format, are added hourly, and total 50 TB. The team runs ad-hoc queries a few times per week. They want to minimize infrastructure management and cost. Which solution is most appropriate?

options.label: A
options.text: Load logs into an RDS PostgreSQL database for SQL analysis requires a bulk import pipeline and RDS cannot efficiently handle ad-hoc analytical queries on large semi-structured datasets.

options.label: B
options.text: Load logs into a Redshift cluster and run SQL queries requires provisioning a dedicated Redshift cluster and loading data first, adding cost and operational overhead for ad-hoc analysis.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Amazon Athena to query JSON logs in S3 directly, with a Glue Data Catalog table definition

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon QuickSight can visualize data but cannot directly query raw JSON log files in S3; it requires a data source like Athena or Redshift to first process the logs.

distractors.A: Load logs into an RDS PostgreSQL database for SQL analysis requires a bulk import pipeline and RDS cannot efficiently handle ad-hoc analytical queries on large semi-structured datasets.
distractors.B: Load logs into a Redshift cluster and run SQL queries requires provisioning a dedicated Redshift cluster and loading data first, adding cost and operational overhead for ad-hoc analysis.
distractors.D: Amazon QuickSight can visualize data but cannot directly query raw JSON log files in S3; it requires a data source like Athena or Redshift to first process the logs.

explanation: Athena queries data directly in S3 using Presto/Trino SQL — no data loading required, no cluster to manage, pay only per query (per TB scanned). A Glue Data Catalog table definition maps to the JSON log structure. For ad-hoc queries a few times per week on 50 TB, Athena is ideal — cost is minimal (a few dollars per query depending on bytes scanned). Redshift requires loading data into the cluster (storage + compute costs, cluster management). RDS is a transactional database, not designed for 50 TB log analytics. QuickSight is a BI visualization tool that uses Athena/Redshift as backends — it doesn't directly replace the query engine.

stem: A company's Lambda function needs to process images uploaded to S3. Processing takes 45 seconds on average. Users experience cold starts of 4-6 seconds for infrequent uploads. The company wants cold start latency below 1 second during business hours (8am-6pm weekday). Which solution achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency during business hours, adjusted via Application Auto Scaling

options.label: B
options.text: Deploy the function in a VPC to access private resources and reduce initialization overhead

options.label: C
options.text: Increase Lambda memory to the maximum (10,240 MB) to reduce initialization time

options.label: D
options.text: Use Lambda SnapStart for the image processing function

explanation: Lambda Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes execution environments, eliminating cold starts. Combined with Application Auto Scaling (scheduled scaling), the provisioned concurrency can be set high during business hours (8am-6pm) and scaled down at night to minimize cost. This guarantees sub-100ms initialization. Increasing memory speeds up CPU-bound execution but doesn't eliminate the cold start initialization phase. VPC deployment adds cold start latency (ENI attachment), it doesn't reduce it. Lambda SnapStart is currently only supported for Java-based functions (using snapshots of initialized environments) — not supported for Python/Node image processing functions.

stem: A company's on-premises NFS file server stores 200 TB of shared files accessed by 500 Windows and Linux users. The company wants to migrate this to AWS and provide the same NFS/SMB protocol access. Which fully managed AWS service meets these requirements?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon S3 with AWS Storage Gateway File Gateway for protocol translation

options.label: B
options.text: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server — managed SMB/NTFS for Windows only

options.label: C
options.text: Amazon EFS — managed NFS accessible from Linux only

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP — supports NFS and SMB simultaneously

explanation: FSx for NetApp ONTAP provides a fully managed shared file system that simultaneously supports NFS (v3, v4.1) and SMB protocols — Windows and Linux clients can access the same file system. It's purpose-built for replacing on-premises NAS appliances. EFS is NFS-only (Linux-focused). FSx for Windows is SMB/NTFS only. Storage Gateway File Gateway translates NFS/SMB to S3 but adds latency and is not a full-performance replacement for 500-concurrent-user NFS/SMB access.

stem: A startup's API has unpredictable traffic that ranges from 0 to 100,000 requests per minute. The team wants to pay only for actual compute used, with no idle costs, and auto-scaling should be transparent. Which compute option is most cost-effective for this traffic pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: Reserved EC2 instances — pre-purchase capacity for peak traffic

options.label: B
options.text: Lambda with API Gateway — function executes per request, scales automatically

options.label: C
options.text: ECS Fargate with Auto Scaling — tasks scale based on CPU/memory metrics

options.label: D
options.text: EC2 with Auto Scaling — scale from 1 to 50 instances based on CPU

explanation: Lambda charges only for actual invocations and duration (100ms billing granularity) — at 0 requests, the cost is $0. This is ideal for unpredictable 0-to-100K RPM traffic. Lambda scales automatically from 0 to 1,000 concurrent executions per region (with quota increases available). EC2 Auto Scaling has a minimum of 1 instance (always-on cost) and scaling takes minutes. Fargate has a minimum task cost and slower scale-out. Reserved EC2 is billed regardless of usage — worst choice for unpredictable traffic.

stem: A company needs to run monthly billing reports that process 500 GB of transaction data from S3. Reports take 3-4 hours to generate using Apache Spark. The reports run once per month. Which compute option minimizes cost for this workload?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS Glue ETL job using built-in Spark for the report generation

options.label: B
options.text: An EMR transient cluster launched with Spot Instances for task nodes and On-Demand

options.label: C
options.text: A persistent EMR cluster running 24/7 with Reserved Instances

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Redshift for running Spark queries against S3 data

explanation: A transient EMR cluster launched only for the monthly 3-4 hour job and terminated immediately after minimizes cost: you pay only for the hours the cluster runs. Using Spot for task nodes (which process data in parallel and can be replaced if interrupted) plus a small On-Demand master/core minimizes per-hour cost. Total monthly cost: ~5 hours × cluster hourly rate × Spot savings. A persistent 24/7 EMR cluster wastes compute for 99.5% of the month. Redshift is a data warehouse, not a Spark runtime. Glue ETL charges per DPU-hour and has job overhead — EMR gives more direct Spark control and Spot pricing.

stem: A company serves video content from CloudFront. Users report that recently updated videos still show the old version for up to 24 hours because of CloudFront caching (TTL=86400). The team needs updated videos to be visible within 5 minutes without reducing the TTL for all content. Which solution achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement versioned S3 object keys (e.g., video-v2.mp4) and update the application to use the new URL

options.label: B
options.text: Enable Origin Shield to improve cache hit ratio and reduce TTL sensitivity

options.label: C
options.text: Reduce the default TTL on the CloudFront distribution to 300 seconds

options.label: D
options.text: Use CloudFront cache invalidations for specific video paths after each upload

explanation: Versioned keys (including a version identifier or hash in the filename) ensure CloudFront never serves the old cached file for the new URL — users requesting `video-v2.mp4` get the new content immediately (cache miss → origin fetch). The application updates the video URL after each upload. This eliminates the invalidation workflow entirely. Reducing global TTL to 300s increases origin load for all content. Cache invalidations work but cost $0.005 per path (cost at scale) and have propagation delays of 10-60 seconds. Origin Shield improves cache hit rates but doesn't solve the stale content problem.

stem: A company runs Apache Kafka on EC2 for a real-time data streaming platform. The operational team spends significant time managing Kafka brokers, ZooKeeper, replication, and upgrades. The company wants to reduce operational overhead while maintaining Kafka-compatible producer/consumer APIs. Which AWS service should they migrate to?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) — fully managed Kafka service

options.label: B
options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams — fully managed, high-throughput data streaming

options.label: C
options.text: Amazon EventBridge — fully managed event bus for application integration

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon SQS FIFO — fully managed message queuing with ordering guarantees

explanation: Amazon MSK is fully managed Apache Kafka — AWS handles broker provisioning, ZooKeeper management, patches, replication, and availability. Existing Kafka producers/consumers work without modification because MSK runs actual Apache Kafka. Migrating to Kinesis requires rewriting all producer/consumer code (different API, different SDK). SQS FIFO is a message queue with different semantics than Kafka streams — not API-compatible. EventBridge is for event routing between AWS services and SaaS — not a Kafka replacement.
